But the loop continues to lower k; if any lower k happens to have at least one non-failure trial (possible with MC randomness), |A|_cutoff would not be updated, but if another lower k also fails uniformly, |A|_cutoff would be overwritten to a smaller value, which contradicts the intended semantics of 'minimum cardinality that still works.' The algorithm needs either a break upon the first failing k or a different assignment rule. This is a reproducibility concern — the intended behavior can be guessed, but the pseudocode as written is genuinely ambiguous.

comments.title: Monte Carlo aspect of Algorithm 1 is not actually specified
comments.quote: the proposed algorithm, as discussed in Algorithm 1, uses Monte Carlo method to compute the cut-off cardinality.
comments.explanation: The text motivates Algorithm 1 as a Monte Carlo method to avoid enumerating the full power set (exponential in |A|). However, the pseudocode says 'until all necessary training action sets for a given cardinality is exhausted,' which sounds like an exhaustive enumeration over subsets of size k, not a sampling procedure. There is no specification of how many subsets are sampled, from what distribution, or what 'necessary' means. For a reader trying to reproduce the method, the 'Monte Carlo' nature of the algorithm is left implicit and the sampling procedure is not defined. Given that the central claim is reducing exponential complexity to polynomial, this is a substantive gap.

comments.title: Shapley formula referenced but not shown
comments.quote: From game theory, _φ_ can be expressed as shown in Equation 1:
comments.explanation: The text introduces 'Equation 1' for the Shapley value formulation, but no equation appears in the passage that follows — it jumps directly to 'where, the sum is over all subsets of A not containing i and C is an arbitrary constant.' The equation is missing from the passage (likely an OCR/extraction issue, but worth flagging since the Shapley formula is central to the paper's methodology). Also, the standard Shapley value does not include an 'arbitrary constant C' — it is uniquely determined by the efficiency, symmetry, linearity/additivity, and null player axioms. Mentioning an arbitrary constant suggests a non-standard formulation that would need explicit justification.

comments.title: Claim of polynomial time complexity not substantiated
comments.quote: It also assists in recognizing cut-off cardinality for the training action space to reduce unnecessary exploration and ensure polynomial time complexity.
comments.explanation: The paper claims the cut-off cardinality reduces Shapley computation from exponential to polynomial time. However, if the cut-off cardinality k* is a constant fraction of |A|, the power set of actions above the cut-off is still exponential in (|A| - k*). For the case study, starting with |A|=5 and cut-off=4 reduces 32 subsets to 6, which is a constant-factor reduction on a tiny instance, not an asymptotic polynomial-time guarantee. The 'polynomial time complexity' claim is not derived or justified; it requires that |A|-k* be O(log |A|) or similar, which is not established. This is an overclaim.

comments.title: Search space reduction percentage: 80% vs 81.25%
comments.quote: It reduces the search space by 80%
comments.explanation: The abstract states the search space is reduced by 80%, while Section 3 (shown in the broader context) states '81.25% reduction in the search space' (from 32 subsets down to 6 sets, i.e., (32-6)/32 = 81.25%). The abstract's '80%' is a reasonable rounding, but technically inconsistent. This is minor but a careful reader comparing abstract to results section would notice.

comments.title: 'Minimum cardinality' vs algorithm's actual output
comments.quote: The cut-off cardinality is defined as the minimium number of action points needed in a training action set
comments.explanation: I want to double-check: the definition says cut-off is the MINIMUM number of action points needed. In the case study, the cut-off is 4, meaning sets of size ≥ 4 are evaluated. This is consistent — 4 is the minimum size at which the agent achieves acceptable performance. But in Algorithm 1, when failure occurs at cardinality k, the algorithm sets |A|_cutoff = k+1, meaning the cut-off is one above the failure threshold. So the cut-off is the smallest k for which NOT all subsets fail. This matches the definition, but the prose framing 'action points only above the cut-off cardinality' in Section 2 is slightly ambiguous — it should be 'at or above' to include the cut-off itself. Minor phrasing concern.

comments.title: Contradiction about which action points Algorithm 2 processes
comments.quote: As for inputs, it takes the combinations of training state-action pairs above the cut-off cardinality, determined by Algorithm 1
comments.explanation: Initially I thought this was a simple statement, but checking the Algorithm 2 input specification itself says: 'All combinations of training state-action pairs upto cut-off cardinality'. The prose says 'above' the cut-off cardinality while the formal algorithm input says 'upto' (up to) the cut-off cardinality. The summary of the paper also says the second algorithm 'takes action points only above the cut-off cardinality'. These are directly contradictory — 'above' and 'up to' describe opposite regions of the cardinality range. A reader cannot determine which subsets Algorithm 2 actually enumerates, which is a reproducibility issue for the core methodology.

comments.title: Cut-off cardinality direction of computational efficiency is unclear/likely inverted
comments.quote: The cut-off cardinality signifies computational efficiency — a larger cut-off cadinality means lower computational overhead and vice versa.
comments.explanation: I paused on this. The cut-off cardinality is defined as the minimum number of action points needed for high-fidelity predictions. In Algorithm 2, subsets at/above (or up to) this cardinality are evaluated combinatorially. If the cut-off is larger, fewer subsets of A remain to enumerate — so 'larger cut-off ⇒ lower overhead' could be defended in that sense. However, the Shapley-style valuation requires iterating over subsets of the power set; larger cut-off means considering subsets of larger size, which generally means more — not fewer — coalitions to evaluate in the combinatorial sum, and each RL training with more actions is itself more expensive. The claim is stated without justification, and given the contradictory 'above' vs 'upto' framing in §2.2, a reader cannot resolve which interpretation is correct. This is an unjustified claim about computational cost that directly affects how readers understand the purpose of Algorithm 1.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 success/failure logic doesn't match the prose description
comments.quote: If for a certain cardinality, all the cumulative rewards do not improve by _ϵ_ within _n_ iterations, then we set the status to failure, and assign the cut-off cardinality to that cardinality plus one.
comments.explanation: I tried to reconcile this with the pseudocode. The prose says: failure happens when 'all the cumulative rewards do not improve' — suggesting status is a single decision per cardinality. But the pseudocode has an inner repeat loop over 'all necessary training action sets for a given cardinality' that sets status success/failure per iteration, and then checks 'if all statuses for a given cardinality, k, are failures'. The logic is that the cutoff is set to k+1 when cardinality k fails entirely. But the loop goes from |A| down to 1 — meaning once failure is detected at some k, the algorithm should break out; otherwise, it will keep iterating down and potentially overwrite |A|cutoff at smaller k values. No break statement is present, so |A|cutoff will end up being set to (smallest-failing-k)+1 rather than (largest-failing-k)+1. Also, 'status' is reassigned each inner iteration and the prior value is not accumulated — the 'if all statuses … are failures' check cannot work as written because statuses are not stored. This is an underspecified/incorrect pseudocode that a reader could not directly implement.

comments.title: Equation 1 referenced but not shown in the passage
comments.quote:
From game theory, _φ_ can be expressed as shown in Equation 1:

where, the sum is over all subsets of _A_ not containing _i_ and _C_ is an arbitrary constant.
comments.explanation: The text introduces Equation 1 and then immediately discusses its terms ('the sum is over all subsets of A not containing i and C is an arbitrary constant') — but no equation appears between the colon and the explanation. This looks like the equation was dropped in the OCR or PDF. Even allowing for OCR artifacts, the standard Shapley formula involves a weighted sum with |S|!(n−|S|−1)!/n! weighting, not just an 'arbitrary constant C'. If C is meant to be this combinatorial weight depending on |S|, calling it an 'arbitrary constant' is incorrect — the Shapley value's axiomatic properties (nullity, symmetry, linearity, efficiency) depend on these specific weights, not an arbitrary constant. This is a genuine concern regardless of OCR: either the equation is missing (making the section unreproducible) or the description of C is technically wrong.

comments.title: Indispensability test in Algorithm 2 uses ablation but notation suggests otherwise
comments.quote: **if** Threshold condition is _never_ satisfied _within_ the acceptable number of iterations **then** _V_ ( _**A** − ai_ ) _← indispensable_
comments.explanation: Reading the prose, 'A training action set is deemed to be indispensable iff upon removal of that training action point, the RL agent fails to accomplish the goal'. This is a leave-one-out ablation test: train with A \ {a_i}, if fails, a_i is indispensable. However, the pseudocode body does π* ← R(S, A, Pa, Ra) — training on the full set A, not on A − a_i. So the condition being tested doesn't match the ablation logic described in prose. Either the training call should use A \ {a_i}, or the logic is described incorrectly. This is a real gap for reproducibility — a reader cannot tell whether the algorithm is trained on the full action set or on the reduced set. Given Table 4 shows rewards for different training action sets that appear to be leave-one-out ablations, the intent seems to be A \ {a_i}, making the pseudocode inconsistent with both the prose and the experimental results.

comments.title: Inconsistency between cut-off cardinality claim and analyzed action sets
comments.quote: Using Algorithm 1, we notice that the power set below the cut-off cardinality of 4 produces trivial and unstable results. Therefore, WLOG, the analysis in this paper has been focused on six training action sets as shown in Table 4.
comments.explanation: The authors state the cut-off cardinality is 4, which per Algorithm 1's definition means the minimum action set size for good performance is 4. However, Table 4 includes a set of cardinality 5 (all five actions) and five sets of cardinality 4 — that's 6 sets total, which matches 'six training action sets'. This is consistent with sets at or above cardinality 4. However, the claimed '81.25% reduction in the search space' needs scrutiny: the power set of 5 elements has 32 subsets (or 31 non-empty). Sets of size ≥4 number C(5,4)+C(5,5) = 5+1 = 6. The reduction would be (32-6)/32 = 81.25% or (31-6)/31 ≈ 80.6%. The 81.25% figure matches 26/32, which does include the empty set in the denominator — a minor definitional quirk but the arithmetic is coherent. The abstract claims '80%' reduction, which roughly matches. No serious inconsistency here, but worth noting the count includes the empty set.

comments.title: Broken reference to Algorithm for step-size determination
comments.quote: Using Algorithm **??** , the optimal step-size for action update is identified to be 0 _._ 1 in both # of vCPUs and Memory Size (GB) dimensions.
comments.explanation: The passage references 'Algorithm ??' — an unresolved LaTeX reference. The paper describes only Algorithm 1 (cut-off cardinality) and Algorithm 2 (categorization/ranking), neither of which determines an optimal step-size for action updates. This leaves the method for identifying the 0.1 step-size completely unspecified. A reader cannot reproduce how this crucial hyperparameter was chosen, and it is not clear which algorithm (if any in the paper) supports this claim. This is an underspecified method issue, not merely a broken cross-reference, because the step-size determination procedure itself appears absent from the paper.

comments.title: Euclidean distance calculation for xlarge t3a vs 2xlarge t3a
comments.quote: While the Euclidean distance between _small t3a_ and _medium t3a_ is equal to 2, that between _xlarge t3a_ and _2xlarge t3a_ is 16 _._ 5.
comments.explanation: Let me check: small t3a = (2,2), medium t3a = (2,4), so Euclidean distance = √(0² + 2²) = 2. ✓ Correct. xlarge t3a = (4,16), 2xlarge t3a = (8,32), so Euclidean distance = √((8-4)² + (32-16)²) = √(16 + 256) = √272 ≈ 16.49. ✓ Correct (rounded to 16.5). The computation is correct.

comments.title: Figure 4 caption says 'three training actions' but means three training action sets
comments.quote: Figure 4 shows the RL loop action with three training actions: _<small t3a, medium t3a, large t3a, xlarge t3a, 2xlarge t3a>_ , _<medium t3a, large t3a, xlarge t3a, 2xlarge t3a>_ , and _<small t3a, large t3a, xlarge t3a, 2xlarge t3a>_ .
comments.explanation: The sentence says 'three training actions' but then lists three training action *sets* (each containing 4–5 actions). A reader could briefly be confused since 'training action' has a specific meaning in this paper (individual action points). Context makes the intent clear, so this is only a minor ambiguity.

comments.title: Reward for Figure 4(c) inconsistent between text and figure caption
comments.quote: For the third training action set of _<small t3a, large t3a, xlarge t3a, 2xlarge t3a>_ , the reward is _−_ 16 as shown in Figure 4(c).
comments.explanation: The main text states the reward for the third action set is −16, which matches Table 4. However, the Figure 4 caption (at the bottom of the passage context) ends with 'The reward is noted to be _−_ 1' — this appears to be a truncated or incorrect value (should presumably be −16 to match the text and Table 4). This could be an OCR cutoff of '−16', in which case it's benign, but if it truly reads '−1' in the paper, it's a contradiction with both the text and Table 4. Given it appears at a line break and the figure caption, OCR truncation is plausible. Flagging as minor on the assumption it is likely an OCR artifact but worth the authors verifying.

comments.title: Parametric boundary described as 'trapezium' with listed vertices
comments.quote:
the parametric boundary which is defined by the trapezium with vertices:

- _{(2,2), (2,8), (8,32), (8,2)}_ in the (# of vCPUs, Memory Size (GB)) space.
comments.explanation: The five EC2 instances from Table 2 are: (2,2), (2,4), (2,8), (4,16), (8,32). The stated vertices of the 'parametric boundary' are {(2,2), (2,8), (8,32), (8,2)}. However, (8,2) is NOT one of the training action points — it is not in Table 2. Furthermore, (4,16) is a training action but is not listed as a vertex. This is suspicious: if the parametric boundary is meant to be the convex hull of training action points, it should be derived from the five training points, and (8,2) should not appear while (4,16) might. Checking the convex hull of {(2,2), (2,4), (2,8), (4,16), (8,32)}: going counterclockwise from (2,2), the hull vertices would be (2,2), (8,32), (2,8) — with (4,16) on the edge from (2,8) to (8,32) (let me verify: line from (2,8) to (8,32) has slope 24/6=4, at x=4 gives y=8+4·2=16. Yes, (4,16) is on this edge). And (2,4) is on the edge from (2,2) to (2,8). So the convex hull is actually a triangle with vertices (2,2), (2,8), (8,32), not a trapezium — and certainly does not include (8,2). This is either a real error in the vertex list (introducing a fictitious point (8,2)) or an undocumented extension of the parameter space. Either way it is underspecified/incorrect.

comments.title: Overclaim that Shapley value has 'not been used' for RL training action valuation
comments.quote: To the best of our knowledge, Shapley value has not been used to quantify training action valuation in a reinforcement learning context.
comments.explanation: This is a novelty claim. It is qualified by 'To the best of our knowledge', so it is appropriately hedged. However, there is a growing literature on Shapley-based credit assignment in multi-agent RL (e.g., Shapley Q-value decomposition). The claim specifically concerns 'training action valuation' (not credit assignment among agents), so the distinction may hold, but the phrasing risks misleading readers unfamiliar with the nuance. Minor overclaim concern, partially mitigated by the hedge.

comments.title: Grammatical error obscures meaning in Related Work
comments.quote: That is because the problem of optimal action selection has relatively more expensive in the cloud infrastructure space where the state-action relationship is only partially observable.
comments.explanation: The sentence 'has relatively more expensive' is ungrammatical ('has' should likely be 'is'). While typically I would not flag grammar, this sentence contains the key justification for why the authors chose the cloud case study rather than standard RL benchmarks. The meaning is recoverable (probably: 'is relatively more expensive'), but the sentence as-written is ambiguous — 'more expensive' in what sense? Computationally? In real dollar terms? This isn't a pure typo issue; the substantive justification is underspecified.

comments.title: Inconsistent reward value for third training action set in Figure 4 caption
comments.quote: (c) RL loop with a training action set of _<small t3a, large t3a, xlarge t3a, 2xlarge t3a>_ . The reward is noted to be _−_ 1
comments.explanation: The caption for Figure 4 in section 2 ends with 'The reward is noted to be −1' for the third training action set <small t3a, large t3a, xlarge t3a, 2xlarge t3a>. However, Table 4 and the main text in Section 3 both state that this action set yields a reward of −16. The passage to check (which repeats the same Figure 4 caption) shows it as '−16', so this appears to be an OCR truncation or a discrepancy between two versions of the caption. If the printed paper actually shows '−1' anywhere, it would be a numerical inconsistency with Table 4 (which reports −16). Given the conflicting captions, this is worth flagging as a localized numerical inconsistency.

comments.title: Overclaim of being 'completely agnostic' of use cases and ML algorithms
comments.quote: Additionally, the proposed methodology is completely agnostic of use cases and machine learning algorithms.
comments.explanation: The conclusion claims the methodology is 'completely agnostic of use cases and machine learning algorithms.' This is a strong claim supported only by a single case study (cloud EC2 resource tuning with an MDP-based RL agent using time-series autoregression and a PID controller). The paper has not demonstrated applicability across multiple domains or RL algorithms empirically; the algorithmic formulation is general, but 'completely agnostic' overstates what has been shown. A more accurate phrasing would be that the methodology is 'designed to be' algorithm- and use-case-agnostic. This is a framing overclaim in a conclusion section.

comments.title: Claim about indispensability of xlarge t3a and 2xlarge t3a not directly evidenced in shown figure
comments.quote: Figure 5: RL loop with _<small t3a, medium t3a, xlarge t3a, 2xlarge t3a>_ action space. The agent could never accomplish the goal, therefore, _large t3a_ is an indispensable element in the training action space. Similarly, _xlarge t3a_ and _2xlarge t3a_ are two indispensable elements in the training action space.
comments.explanation: Figure 5 illustrates only the removal of large t3a (the action set <small t3a, medium t3a, xlarge t3a, 2xlarge t3a>) and shows the goal is not reached — correctly supporting the indispensability of large t3a. The caption then asserts 'Similarly, xlarge t3a and 2xlarge t3a are two indispensable elements,' but Figure 5 itself does not display the corresponding failed runs upon their removal. Table 4 does list these action sets as 'none', which supports the claim, so a careful reader can cross-check. The concern is that the caption says 'similarly' as if Figure 5 visualizes all three, when it only visualizes one. Minor ambiguity, not a substantive error since Table 4 supplies the evidence.

progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an interesting and practically relevant problem—how to select or prune the training action space in reinforcement learning—and the proposed Shapley-inspired framing could be a useful direction for data-centric RL/MLOps. However, based on the introduction and algorithm setup, the paper currently lacks sufficient conceptual precision and technical rigor. Key terms such as “training action space,” “training action points,” “cut-off cardinality,” “dispensable/indispensable actions,” and their distinction from the actual RL action space are not clearly formalized, making it difficult to assess what problem is being solved. The connection to Shapley values also appears underdeveloped: the text invokes Shapley-style valuation but does not yet define the characteristic function, coalition structure, marginal contribution estimate, approximation guarantee, or why the proposed Monte Carlo procedure is a principled substitute for Shapley computation. Several claims are broad or insufficiently justified, such as polynomial-time complexity, general extensibility across RL domains, and the statement that larger action spaces can reduce cumulative reward. The writing is understandable at a high level but contains grammatical issues, imprecise notation, and occasional conceptual errors or ambiguities that reduce clarity. The empirical motivation is promising, but a single cloud resource tuning case study may not be enough to support the general claims unless accompanied by strong baselines, ablations, statistical analysis, and comparisons to standard action-space reduction or feature/data valuation methods. Overall, the paper has a potentially valuable applied idea, but it needs clearer problem formulation, a more rigorous algorithmic presentation, stronger justification of the Shapley connection, and broader empirical validation to meet the standards of a top ML conference.

comments.title: Cut-off cardinality does not by itself ensure polynomial-time complexity
comments.quote: It also assists in recognizing cut-off cardinality for the training action space to reduce unnecessary exploration and ensure polynomial time complexity.
comments.explanation: The intended point is plausible: ignoring small or unpromising action subsets can reduce practical computation relative to full power-set enumeration. However, the stronger claim that a cut-off cardinality ensures polynomial-time complexity is not justified. Evaluating all subsets above a threshold c generally requires evaluating \(\sum_{k=c}^{|A|} \binom{|A|}{k}\) subsets, which remains exponential unless c is constrained to be very close to \(|A|\). Algorithm 1 also gives no theorem or bound showing that the number of evaluated subsets is polynomial in \(|A|\). The five-action case study demonstrates a reduction for one small instance, but not a general complexity guarantee.

comments.title: The claimed Monte Carlo reduction is not specified and the algorithm still appears to enumerate subsets
comments.quote: **Algorithm 1** Monte Carlo method for cut-off cardinality computation for the training action space **Input:** The RL algorithm, _R_ , the power set for the training action space, _P_ ( _A_ ), ... **end if until** all necessary training action sets for a given cardinality is exhausted.
comments.explanation: The paper says Algorithm 1 uses a Monte Carlo method to avoid unnecessary exploration, but the algorithm does not specify the Monte Carlo components needed for reproduction: what is sampled, from what distribution, how many samples are drawn, what estimator is used, or what confidence/error criterion determines termination. Instead, the formal input includes the full power set and the stopping condition refers to exhausting all necessary training action sets for a cardinality, which sounds closer to enumeration. As written, the procedure could still require exponentially many subset evaluations, so the source of the claimed computational saving is unclear.

comments.title: Cut-off cardinality and reward-improvement criteria are not formally defined
comments.quote: The cut-off cardinality is defined as the minimium number of action points needed in a training action set to enable high-fidelity predictions within an acceptable computational complexity and an error bound.
comments.explanation: The definition invokes high-fidelity predictions, acceptable computational complexity, and an error bound, but does not specify the prediction target, fidelity metric, or type of error being bounded. Later, Algorithm 1 appears to use a reward-improvement test: whether cumulative reward improves by \(\epsilon\) within \(n\) iterations. That criterion is also undefined because the baseline for improvement is not stated: it could refer to the initial reward, previous iteration, full action set, previous cardinality, or a task-specific threshold. Since RL training may be stochastic, the paper should also define how rewards are aggregated across runs and how random variation is handled.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 can overwrite the cut-off cardinality unless it stops after the first failure boundary
comments.quote: **for** _k ←|A|_ to 1 **do repeat** _π[∗] ←R_ ( _S, A, Pa, Ra_ ) **if** the cumulative reward improves by _ϵ_ with _n_ iterations **then** _status ← success_ **else** _status ← failure_ **end if until** all necessary training action sets for a given cardinality is exhausted. **if** all statuses for a given cardinality, _k_ , are _failures_ **then** _|A|cutoff ← k_ + 1 **end if end for**
comments.explanation: The intended logic seems to be: start with the full action space, decrease cardinality, and when a cardinality becomes insufficient, set the cut-off to one larger. But the pseudocode does not stop after assigning the cut-off. If all subsets of size 3 fail and the intended cut-off is 4, then all subsets of size 2 and 1 may also fail, causing later assignments to 3 and then 2. Without a `break`, `return`, or explicit max/min convention, the algorithm's output is ambiguous and may be wrong.

comments.title: The training subset used at each cardinality is not specified in the RL call
comments.quote: **for** _k ←|A|_ to 1 **do repeat** _π[∗] ←R_ ( _S, A, Pa, Ra_ )
comments.explanation: The prose indicates that the agent is trained using different training action subsets, but the pseudocode repeatedly calls the RL algorithm with the full action space _A_. The algorithm should explicitly pass a subset such as \(A' \subseteq A\) of cardinality k, and should define how the corresponding states, transitions, and rewards are restricted or re-estimated. Without this, the cut-off cardinality computation is not reproducible.

comments.title: Algorithm 2 gives contradictory directions about whether to use subsets above or below the cut-off
comments.quote: **Input:** All combinations of training state-action pairs upto cut-off cardinality: _{_ ( _si, ai_ ) _}_
comments.explanation: The surrounding text says Algorithm 2 uses combinations above the cut-off cardinality, and the case study with cut-off 4 evaluates cardinalities 4 and 5. But the formal input says combinations "upto cut-off cardinality," which normally means cardinalities less than or equal to the cut-off. This changes both the computational cost and the categorization results. The paper should define whether the retained family is \(|A'| \ge |A|_{cutoff}\), \(|A'| > |A|_{cutoff}\), or \(|A'| \le |A|_{cutoff}\).

comments.title: Algorithm 2 conflates action sets, individual actions, and state-action pairs
comments.quote: The dispensable action points are rank listed in _L_ based on the corresponding cumulative reward.
comments.explanation: The method alternates between evaluating action sets, classifying individual action points, and looping over state-action pairs without defining the mapping among these objects. Cardinality and cumulative reward are properties of an evaluated action set, while the stated output is a ranking or categorization of individual actions. The pseudocode also assigns labels to _V(A-a_i)_, the remaining action set, rather than to the removed action _a_i_, and loops over _<s_i,a_i>_ even though removing one state-action sample is not necessarily equivalent to removing an action. To rank individual dispensable actions, the paper needs a defined score, such as leave-one-out reward, mean reward over retained coalitions, or a Shapley-style marginal contribution.

comments.title: The Shapley-value normalization cannot be an arbitrary constant
comments.quote: where, the sum is over all subsets of _A_ not containing _i_ and _C_ is an arbitrary constant. We call _φi_ the Action Shapley value of action _ai_ .
comments.explanation: In the standard Shapley formula, the weights are not arbitrary: the marginal contribution for subset _S_ is weighted by \(|S|!(|A|-|S|-1)!/|A|!\). These weights are required for the usual Shapley axioms, especially efficiency. A global arbitrary constant may preserve some qualitative properties, but it does not give the standard Shapley value unless the normalization is chosen correctly. If the equation is intended to be standard Shapley valuation, _C_ should not be described as arbitrary.

comments.title: The PCA and time-series modeling complexity claim is not justified by the stated variables
comments.quote: The state-action mapping and transition probability are modeled using time-series auto-regression [38] after a PCA-based dimensionality reduction [41] with time complexity _O_ ( _klogk_ ), where _k_ is the number of principal components.
comments.explanation: The stated complexity depends only on the number of principal components, but standard PCA complexity also depends on the number of samples and the original feature dimension, and different exact or randomized PCA algorithms have different costs. Fitting an autoregressive time-series model also depends on the series length and lag order. Unless the paper uses a specific approximate PCA and AR implementation with a proven \(O(k\log k)\) bound, this complexity statement is not generally valid.

comments.title: Using “WLOG” overstates that the chosen action-update algorithm is immaterial
comments.quote: For action updates, WLOG, an RL-based PID controller [1, 13] is used. Similar to time-series modeling, action update can be conducted by other policy learning algorithms such as SARSA [19].
comments.explanation: The framework may be intended to be algorithm-agnostic, but "without loss of generality" is stronger than justified. The valuation and ranking can depend on the learning algorithm, exploration behavior, controller dynamics, and convergence properties. Unless the authors show that the categorization is invariant to the action-update method, this should be presented as an implementation choice for the case study rather than a WLOG assumption.

comments.title: The case-study cut-off cardinality of 4 is asserted without reproducible evidence
comments.quote: Using Algorithm 1, we notice that the power set below the cut-off cardinality of 4 produces trivial and unstable results. Therefore, WLOG, the analysis in this paper has been focused on six training action sets as shown in Table 4.
comments.explanation: The cut-off of 4 is central because it reduces the evaluated subsets to the five leave-one-out sets plus the full set. However, the paper does not show the Algorithm 1 outcomes for cardinalities 1, 2, and 3; does not state how many Monte Carlo trials were run; does not quantify what "trivial and unstable" means; and does not demonstrate that all sets of a failed cardinality failed according to the algorithm's reward-improvement criterion. Calling this restriction WLOG is also inappropriate because excluding lower-cardinality action sets is a substantive empirical choice.

comments.title: Search-space reduction percentage appears to count the invalid empty action set
comments.quote: Therefore, WLOG, the analysis in this paper has been focused on six training action sets as shown in Table 4. That amounts to 81.25% reduction in the search space.
comments.explanation: Keeping all subsets of cardinality at least 4 from five actions gives \(\binom{5}{4}+\binom{5}{5}=6\) subsets. The stated 81.25% reduction is correct only if the denominator is the full power set size \(2^5=32\), since \((32-6)/32=81.25\%\). But Algorithm 1 iterates from \(|A|\) down to 1 and the empty action set is not a meaningful RL training action set. If the relevant search space is the 31 nonempty subsets, the reduction is \((31-6)/31\approx 80.65\%\).

comments.title: Step-size selection is not reproducible and refers to an unresolved algorithm
comments.quote: Using Algorithm **??** , the optimal step-size for action update is identified to be 0 _._ 1 in both # of vCPUs and Memory Size (GB) dimensions.
comments.explanation: The unresolved algorithm reference is methodological, not merely typographical, because the action-update step size affects RL/PID trajectories, step counts, and the rewards reported in Table 4. The paper does not specify the objective used to call 0.1 optimal, the candidate step sizes considered, whether tuning and evaluation used the same data, or whether the step size was chosen separately for different action sets. Without these details, the reported rewards and rankings are difficult to reproduce.

comments.title: The continuous action-update model and feasible boundary are not reconciled with the discrete EC2 action set
comments.quote: the optimal step-size for action update is identified to be 0 _._ 1 in both # of vCPUs and Memory Size (GB) dimensions.
comments.explanation: The case study appears to model actions in a continuous space of vCPUs and memory, using fractional update steps such as 0.1. But the listed EC2 configurations are discrete instance types with fixed CPU and memory values. The stated trapezium boundary also includes the point \((8,2)\), which is not one of the listed EC2 action points and creates a broader feasible-looking region than the observed configurations. This could be acceptable if the controller operates in a continuous surrogate and then projects or rounds to valid EC2 types, but the paper does not specify such a mapping. As written, it is unclear how continuous recommendations are converted into executable cloud actions.

comments.title: Ranking claims are based on single reported rewards without uncertainty or repeatability details
comments.quote: With this set up, different RL models are developed with different training action sets and the corresponding rewards and ranks are noted in Table 4.
comments.explanation: The table supports the literal ordering of the reported reward values, but the paper does not say whether these rewards are averages over repeated runs, single deterministic simulations, or single empirical trials. RL training and cloud measurements can be stochastic or noisy, so rankings based on single values may be unstable. Since the paper uses these rewards to argue that one training action set is best and to motivate action valuation, it should report variance, confidence intervals, random seeds, repeated trials, or evidence that the procedure is deterministic.

progressive_original__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive Original (gpt-5.5)
progressive_original__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper addresses an interesting and practically relevant problem—how to select or prune the training action space in reinforcement learning—and the proposed Shapley-inspired framing could be a useful direction for data-centric RL/MLOps. However, based on the introduction and algorithm setup, the paper currently lacks sufficient conceptual precision and technical rigor. Key terms such as “training action space,” “training action points,” “cut-off cardinality,” “dispensable/indispensable actions,” and their distinction from the actual RL action space are not clearly formalized, making it difficult to assess what problem is being solved. The connection to Shapley values also appears underdeveloped: the text invokes Shapley-style valuation but does not yet define the characteristic function, coalition structure, marginal contribution estimate, approximation guarantee, or why the proposed Monte Carlo procedure is a principled substitute for Shapley computation. Several claims are broad or insufficiently justified, such as polynomial-time complexity, general extensibility across RL domains, and the statement that larger action spaces can reduce cumulative reward. The writing is understandable at a high level but contains grammatical issues, imprecise notation, and occasional conceptual errors or ambiguities that reduce clarity. The empirical motivation is promising, but a single cloud resource tuning case study may not be enough to support the general claims unless accompanied by strong baselines, ablations, statistical analysis, and comparisons to standard action-space reduction or feature/data valuation methods. Overall, the paper has a potentially valuable applied idea, but it needs clearer problem formulation, a more rigorous algorithmic presentation, stronger justification of the Shapley connection, and broader empirical validation to meet the standards of a top ML conference.

comments.title: Cut-off cardinality does not by itself ensure polynomial-time complexity
comments.quote: It also assists in recognizing cut-off cardinality for the training action space to reduce unnecessary exploration and ensure polynomial time complexity.
comments.explanation: I understand the intended point: if the method can ignore many small or unpromising action subsets, it can reduce the practical number of RL trainings relative to a full Shapley-style enumeration over the power set. However, the stronger claim that this "ensure[s] polynomial time complexity" is not justified by the surrounding context. Even if only subsets above a cut-off cardinality c are evaluated, the number of such subsets is generally \(\sum_{k=c}^{|A|} \binom{|A|}{k}\), which remains exponential unless c is constrained to be very close to \(|A|\), e.g. \(|A|-O(1)\). Later Algorithm 1 also appears to evaluate action sets by cardinality until a stopping condition, but no theorem or bound is given showing that the number of evaluated subsets is polynomial in \(|A|\). The case study demonstrates a reduction for five actions, but that does not establish the stated complexity guarantee.

comments.title: Monte Carlo procedure is asserted but not specified enough to reproduce or justify
comments.quote: To circumvent this problem, the proposed algorithm, as discussed in Algorithm 1, uses Monte Carlo method to compute the cut-off cardinality.
comments.explanation: The motivation is sensible: a Monte Carlo approximation could reduce the cost of estimating Shapley-like quantities or identifying a useful cardinality threshold. But after checking the later algorithmic context, the actual Monte Carlo components are not made explicit. A reproducible Monte Carlo method would normally specify what is sampled, from what distribution, how many samples are drawn per cardinality, what estimator is used, and what confidence/error criterion determines termination. The later pseudocode instead refers to exhausting "all necessary training action sets" for a cardinality, which sounds closer to enumeration than Monte Carlo sampling. Thus the reader cannot tell whether the claimed computational saving comes from random sampling, deterministic pruning, or case-specific early stopping.

comments.title: Cut-off cardinality definition is not tied to a formal performance or error metric
comments.quote: The cut-off cardinality is defined as the minimium number of action points needed in a training action set to enable high-fidelity predictions within an acceptable computational complexity and an error bound.
comments.explanation: The authors are trying to define a practical threshold below which action sets are too small or unstable, which is a useful idea. The difficulty is that the definition invokes "high-fidelity predictions" and "an error bound" without specifying the prediction target, fidelity metric, or statistical/computational error being bounded. In the surrounding paper, the operational criteria appear to involve cumulative reward improvement, a threshold condition, and a finite number of iterations, not prediction fidelity in the usual supervised-learning sense. Unless the authors formally connect reward-based success to a prediction error bound, the definition is ambiguous and hard to reproduce.

comments.title: The method alternates between ranking action sets and ranking individual actions
comments.quote: The second algorithm takes action points only above the cut-off cardinality and categorize action points into two classes: _{dispensable, indispensable}_ and ranking dispensable training action points based on the corresponding cumulative rewards.
comments.explanation: I can see the intended Shapley-style idea: evaluate how removing or including an action affects the trained agent, then use those effects to classify or rank actions. However, the wording here conflates several objects. Cardinality is a property of action sets, cumulative reward is observed for a trained model using a particular action set, while the output is described as a ranking of individual action points. Later tables also report rewards and ranks for action sets, whereas categorization is reported for individual actions. Without a clearly defined marginal contribution, aggregation rule over coalitions, or Shapley estimator, the reader cannot infer how a cumulative reward assigned to a set becomes a unique ranking score for a single dispensable action. This is central to the proposed valuation methodology.

comments.title: MDP/objective definition omits horizon or discounting needed for a well-defined long-term return
comments.quote: An RL problem is defined by a quartet of ( _S, A, Pa, Ra_ ), where _S_ is a set of states or the state space; _A_ is a set of actions or the action space available to influence _S_ ; _Pa_ ( _s, s[′]_ ) = _Pr_ ( _st_ +1 = _s[′] |st_ = _s, at_ = _a_ ) is the transition probability which is the probability that action _a_ in state _s_ at time _t_ will lead to state _s[′]_ at time _t_ + 1; and finally, _Ra_ ( _s, s[′]_ ) is the immediate reward signal received after transitioning from state _s_ to state _s[′]_ , due to action _a_ . An RL agent training involves recognizing an optimal policy function, _π[∗]_ : _a ← s_ , from a corpus of _{_ ( _si, ai_ ) _}_ to maximize the long-term cumulative reward,[] _Ra_ .
comments.explanation: The transition and reward notation is mostly interpretable as an action-indexed MDP kernel and reward function, and an introductory section can reasonably simplify. Still, the objective "long-term cumulative reward" is not fully specified by the quartet \((S,A,P_a,R_a)\). Standard RL formulations usually include either a finite horizon, an absorbing/episodic setup, or a discount factor \(\gamma\) to make the return and optimality criterion well defined. Later parts of the paper use an acceptable number of iterations/steps, which may effectively impose a finite horizon, but that is not included in the formal problem definition here. This is a localized ambiguity rather than a fatal flaw, but it matters because the proposed valuation depends on comparing cumulative rewards across trained agents.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 can overwrite the cut-off cardinality unless it stops after the first failure boundary
comments.quote: **for** _k ←|A|_ to 1 **do repeat** _π[∗] ←R_ ( _S, A, Pa, Ra_ ) **if** the cumulative reward improves by _ϵ_ with _n_ iterations **then** _status ← success_ **else** _status ← failure_ **end if until** all necessary training action sets for a given cardinality is exhausted. **if** all statuses for a given cardinality, _k_ , are _failures_ **then** _|A|cutoff ← k_ + 1 **end if end for**
comments.explanation: I understand the intended logic: start with the full action space, decrease the cardinality, and when a cardinality becomes insufficient, set the cut-off to one larger. That is a reasonable boundary-search idea. However, the pseudocode does not stop after assigning _|A|cutoff_. Since the loop continues down to 1, later smaller cardinalities that also fail would reassign the cut-off again. For example, if all subsets of size 3 fail and the intended cut-off is 4, then all subsets of size 2 and 1 might also fail, causing subsequent assignments to 3 and then 2. This is not just a presentation detail: without a `break`, `return`, or max/min convention, the algorithm’s output is ambiguous and may be wrong.

comments.title: The claimed Monte Carlo reduction is not reflected in the algorithm, which still appears to enumerate subsets
comments.quote: **Algorithm 1** Monte Carlo method for cut-off cardinality computation for the training action space **Input:** The RL algorithm, _R_ , the power set for the training action space, _P_ ( _A_ ), ... **end if until** all necessary training action sets for a given cardinality is exhausted.
comments.explanation: The surrounding prose says the method uses Monte Carlo simulation to avoid unnecessary exploration, which would be a plausible way to approximate a Shapley-style subset evaluation. But the formal inputs include the full power set _P(A)_, and the stopping condition says to continue until all necessary training action sets for a given cardinality are exhausted. No sampling distribution, sample budget, confidence criterion, or estimator is specified. As written, the procedure could still require evaluating exponentially many subsets, especially if “necessary” means all subsets at each cardinality. This leaves the central computational-efficiency claim insufficiently justified.

comments.title: The training subset used at each cardinality is not specified in the RL call
comments.quote: **for** _k ←|A|_ to 1 **do repeat** _π[∗] ←R_ ( _S, A, Pa, Ra_ )
comments.explanation: The prose makes clear that the authors intend to train the RL agent with different numbers of training actions. I therefore expected the algorithm to pass a subset such as _A_k \subseteq A_ and the corresponding state data or transition model into _R_. Instead, the pseudocode repeatedly calls _R(S,A,P_a,R_a)_ with the full action space _A_. If this is only shorthand, the intended subset should still be made explicit, because the cut-off cardinality cannot be computed unless the algorithm specifies which subset is being evaluated at each iteration and how the corresponding _S_, _P_a_, and rewards are restricted or re-estimated.

comments.title: The reward-improvement criterion for Algorithm 1 is undefined
comments.quote: If for a certain cardinality, all the cumulative rewards do not improve by _ϵ_ within _n_ iterations, then we set the status to failure, and assign the cut-off cardinality to that cardinality plus one.
comments.explanation: The intent seems to be to decide whether a training action set is adequate by checking whether learning progress exceeds an acceptable margin. However, it is unclear what “improve by _ϵ_” is measured against: the previous training iteration, the initial reward, the reward from the full action set, the previous cardinality, or a task threshold. This matters especially because the case-study reward is negative time-to-threshold, so improvement means becoming less negative, and because RL training is stochastic. Without a baseline, aggregation rule, and treatment of random variation, two readers could implement different cut-off criteria from the same description.

comments.title: Algorithm 2 contradicts the stated direction of the cut-off cardinality
comments.quote: Algorithm 2 offers ... the combinations of training state-action pairs above the cut-off cardinality, determined by Algorithm 1
comments.explanation: This sentence is consistent with the later case study: a cut-off of 4 leads to evaluating the size-4 and size-5 training action sets. However, the formal input to Algorithm 2 says the opposite.

comments.title: Algorithm 2 input says “upto cut-off cardinality,” which would select the opposite subset family
comments.quote: **Input:** All combinations of training state-action pairs upto cut-off cardinality: _{_ ( _si, ai_ ) _}_
comments.explanation: Taken literally, “upto cut-off cardinality” usually means cardinalities less than or equal to the cut-off. But the preceding paragraph says Algorithm 2 takes combinations above the cut-off cardinality, and the case study with cut-off 4 evaluates cardinalities 4 and 5, not 1 through 4. This is not merely wording: evaluating subsets below versus above the cut-off changes the computational cost and the categorization results. The authors should define whether the retained family is _|A'| \ge |A|_{cutoff}_, _|A'| > |A|_{cutoff}_, or _|A'| \le |A|_{cutoff}_.

comments.title: The categorization vector labels the reduced action set rather than the removed action
comments.quote:
**if** Threshold condition is _never_ satisfied _within_ the acceptable number of iterations **then** _V_ ( _**A** − ai_ ) _← indispensable_

**else** _V_ ( _**A** − ai_ ) _← dispensable_
comments.explanation: The prose defines indispensability in the standard leave-one-out sense: an action point _a_i_ is indispensable if removing it causes failure. That idea is clear and useful. But the pseudocode assigns the label to _V(A-a_i)_, which denotes the remaining action set, not the removed action point. Since _V_ is described as classifying training action points, the assignment should presumably be _V(a_i) ← indispensable_ when training on _A-a_i_ fails. As written, the algorithm confuses whether it is categorizing coalitions or individual actions.

comments.title: The loop variable in Algorithm 2 does not match the leave-one-action test
comments.quote: **repeat for** _< si, ai >_ **do** _π[∗] ←R_ ( _S, A, Pa, Ra_ )
comments.explanation: I infer that the authors want to evaluate action removals or action-set combinations. But the loop is written over individual state-action pairs _<s_i,a_i>_, while the decision rule is about removing an action point from the training action space. In RL, many state observations may correspond to the same action, and removing an action point is not equivalent to iterating over a single state-action sample. The algorithm should specify whether it loops over action points, over all coalitions above the cut-off, or over state-action trajectories associated with each action. Otherwise the categorization procedure is not reproducible.

comments.title: Ranking individual dispensable actions by “corresponding cumulative reward” is underspecified
comments.quote: The dispensable action points are rank listed in _L_ based on the corresponding cumulative reward. Essentially, _L_ informs the performance impact of different disposable action points on the RL agent.
comments.explanation: The goal of ranking dispensable actions is sensible. However, an individual action can be associated with many rewards depending on which coalition it is included in or removed from. Standard Shapley valuation resolves this by averaging marginal contributions over coalitions with specific weights. Here, the algorithm does not define whether the reward assigned to an action is the reward after removing it from the full set, the best/worst/mean reward over retained coalitions, or a Shapley-style marginal contribution. This ambiguity becomes important because Table 4 ranks action sets, whereas the text says _L_ ranks action points.

comments.title: The Shapley-value normalization cannot be an arbitrary constant
comments.quote: where, the sum is over all subsets of _A_ not containing _i_ and _C_ is an arbitrary constant. We call _φi_ the Action Shapley value of action _ai_ .
comments.explanation: The authors are right that Shapley values are a natural reference point for equitable valuation. But in the standard Shapley formula, the weights are not arbitrary: for a player set _A_, the marginal contribution for subset _S_ is weighted by _|S|!(|A|-|S|-1)!/|A|!_. These weights are required for the usual Shapley axioms, especially efficiency. A global constant scaling may preserve some properties such as symmetry and nullity, but it no longer gives the standard Shapley value unless the normalization is chosen correctly. If Equation 1 has the standard weights, the explanation should not describe _C_ as arbitrary.

comments.title: The PCA/time-series modeling complexity claim is not justified by the stated variables
comments.quote: The state-action mapping and transition probability are modeled using time-series auto-regression [38] after a PCA-based dimensionality reduction [41] with time complexity _O_ ( _klogk_ ), where _k_ is the number of principal components.
comments.explanation: It is plausible that the authors use PCA followed by an autoregressive model as an empirical transition model. The concern is the stated complexity. Standard PCA complexity depends on the number of samples and original feature dimension, not only on the number of retained principal components; common exact and randomized variants have different dependencies. Similarly, fitting an autoregressive model has its own dependence on time-series length and lag order. The paper may be using a particular approximate PCA implementation, but that is not specified. As written, _O(k\log k)_ with _k_ only the number of principal components is not a generally valid complexity statement.

comments.title: Use of “WLOG” overstates that the chosen action-update algorithm is immaterial
comments.quote: For action updates, WLOG, an RL-based PID controller [1, 13] is used. Similar to time-series modeling, action update can be conducted by other policy learning algorithms such as SARSA [19].
comments.explanation: The authors reasonably want the framework to be algorithm-agnostic, and they later mention SARSA as an alternative. However, “without loss of generality” is stronger than justified here. The valuation and ranking can depend on the learning algorithm, exploration behavior, controller dynamics, and convergence properties. Unless there is an argument that the categorization is invariant to the action-update method, this should be framed as an implementation choice for the case study rather than WLOG.

comments.title: Cut-off cardinality of 4 is asserted without reproducible evidence
comments.quote: Using Algorithm 1, we notice that the power set below the cut-off cardinality of 4 produces trivial and unstable results. Therefore, WLOG, the analysis in this paper has been focused on six training action sets as shown in Table 4.
comments.explanation: I understand the authors' intent: Algorithm 1 is meant to justify ignoring smaller action subsets, and with five actions a cut-off of 4 naturally leaves the five leave-one-out subsets plus the full set. However, I checked this against the earlier definition of Algorithm 1, where failure is based on whether cumulative reward improves by \(\epsilon\) within \(n\) iterations. The passage instead says the smaller subsets produce "trivial and unstable results," which is not the same criterion and is not quantified. The case-study table gives an error margin and acceptable steps, but the paper does not show the actual Algorithm 1 outcomes for cardinalities 1, 2, and 3, how many Monte Carlo trials were run, what counted as instability, or whether all sets of a given cardinality failed as required by the algorithm. Because the claimed search-space reduction and subsequent categorization depend on this cut-off, the empirical basis for a central methodological step is under-specified. Also, calling the restriction "WLOG" is not quite right: excluding lower-cardinality action sets is a substantive empirical choice, not a lossless simplification.

comments.title: Search-space reduction percentage appears to count the invalid empty action set
comments.quote: The RL training action space is spanned by the power set drawn from the five pairs of EC2 configuration set points as shown in Table 2. Using Algorithm 1, we notice that the power set below the cut-off cardinality of 4 produces trivial and unstable results. Therefore, WLOG, the analysis in this paper has been focused on six training action sets as shown in Table 4. That amounts to 81.25% reduction in the search space.
comments.explanation: The number six is consistent with keeping all subsets of cardinality at least 4 from five actions: \(\binom{5}{4}+\binom{5}{5}=6\). The stated 81.25% reduction is also arithmetically correct if the denominator is the full power set size \(2^5=32\), since \((32-6)/32=81.25\%\). However, Algorithm 1 iterates from \(|A|\) down to 1 and never evaluates the empty action set, which would not be a meaningful RL training action set. If the relevant search space is the nonempty subsets, its size is \(2^5-1=31\), and reducing to six sets is \((31-6)/31\approx 80.65\%\), not 81.25%. This is a small numerical inconsistency, but it matters because the paper frames the reduction percentage as an empirical benefit of the method.

comments.title: Step-size selection is not reproducible and refers to an unresolved algorithm
comments.quote: Using Algorithm **??** , the optimal step-size for action update is identified to be 0 _._ 1 in both # of vCPUs and Memory Size (GB) dimensions.
comments.explanation: At first this might look like a harmless cross-reference problem, but the content is methodological: the action-update step size affects the RL/PID trajectory, the number of steps to threshold, and therefore the reward values in Table 4. In the provided context, only Algorithm 1 and Algorithm 2 are defined, neither of which appears to optimize an action-update step size. The passage also does not specify the objective used to call 0.1 "optimal," the candidate step sizes considered, whether the same data were used for tuning and evaluation, or whether the step size was chosen separately for different action sets. Without these details, a reader cannot reproduce the reported rewards or assess whether the ranking is sensitive to this hyperparameter.

comments.title: Fractional action-update step size conflicts with the discrete EC2 action space unless interpolation is specified
comments.quote: the optimal step-size for action update is identified to be 0 _._ 1 in both # of vCPUs and Memory Size (GB) dimensions.
comments.explanation: The authors seem to model actions in a continuous parameter space \((\#\text{vCPUs}, \text{Memory GB})\), which may be reasonable for a simulator or PID-style controller. But the actual action points in Table 2 are discrete EC2 instance configurations: 2, 4, or 8 vCPUs and fixed memory sizes. A step size of 0.1 vCPUs or 0.1 GB is not directly realizable as an EC2 instance action. This concern would be resolved if the policy operates in a continuous surrogate model and then projects or rounds recommendations to valid EC2 types, or if the continuous trajectory is used only for visualization. The passage does not say this. As written, it is unclear how continuous recommended actions are mapped back to executable cloud actions, which affects both reproducibility and interpretation of the reported step counts.

comments.title: The trapezium boundary includes a configuration not present in the EC2 action set
comments.quote:
the parametric boundary which is defined by the trapezium with vertices:

- _{(2,2), (2,8), (8,32), (8,2)}_ in the (# of vCPUs, Memory Size (GB)) space.
comments.explanation: I checked the vertices against Table 2. The training action points are \((2,2), (2,4), (2,8), (4,16), (8,32)\). The point \((8,2)\) is not one of the EC2 configurations listed, and including it creates a much larger feasible-looking trapezium than the convex hull of the observed EC2 configurations, which would be essentially bounded by \((2,2), (2,8), (8,32)\). The authors may intend a broader continuous parameter boundary using minimum and maximum vCPU/memory values, but that is not stated. This matters because the RL trajectory and the arbitrary initial action \((6,14)\) can appear inside or outside the feasible region depending on this boundary choice. If the action model is allowed to interpolate over an artificial continuous region, the construction and feasibility constraints should be justified.

comments.title: Ranking claims are based on single reported rewards without uncertainty or repeatability details
comments.quote: With this set up, different RL models are developed with different training action sets and the corresponding rewards and ranks are noted in Table 4. Remarkably, a high cardinality training action set does not guarantee the best reward: the agent with all training actions, _<small t3a, medium t3a, large t3a, xlarge t3a, 2xlarge t3a>_ , does not yield the highest reward. In fact, the action set with _<medium t3a, large t3a, xlarge t3a, 2xlarge t3a>_ yields the highest reward.
comments.explanation: The table does support the literal ordering of the reported rewards: \(-13\) is better than \(-16\) and \(-21\) under the paper's negative-step reward definition. However, RL training and cloud measurements can be stochastic or noisy, and the passage does not state whether these rewards are averages over repeated runs, single deterministic simulations, or single empirical trials. If the procedure is fully deterministic after fitting the time-series model, then the ranking may be fine, but that determinism is not established. Since the paper uses these rewards to argue that one training action set is best and to motivate action valuation, the lack of variance, confidence intervals, random seeds, or repeatability information makes the strength of the ranking claim difficult to assess.
